abstract: This research explores the educational philosophy of Imam Al-Ghazali as presented in his seminal work, *Ihya’ Ulumiddin*, and assesses its applicability within the framework of modern pedagogy. As a cornerstone of Islamic intellectual history, Al-Ghazali’s thought posits that education must transcend the simple transmission of empirical data to focus on the holistic cultivation of an individual's spiritual and moral faculties. Utilizing a library research methodology, this study analyzes his perspectives on ethics, the integration of secular and religious knowledge, and the essential role of the educator as a moral archetype. The findings indicate that Al-Ghazali’s emphasis on active, reflective, and character-driven learning offers a robust solution to the ethical voids and purely materialistic orientations often found in contemporary schooling. By bridging traditional spiritual values with modern instructional needs, the paper highlights how Al-Ghazali’s insights into character education and holistic development remain enduringly relevant for fostering a balanced and ethically responsible generation.
